title: John Swinney's talk of US military ban at Prestwick is worst kind of political grandstanding

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: Did you know with a Digital Subscription to The Scotsman, you can get unlimited access to the website including our premium content, as well as benefiting from fewer ads, loyalty rewards and much more. Subscription Offers Sorry, there seem to be some issues. Please try again later. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA and the Google Privacy Notice and Terms of Service apply. As someone who has written about Glasgow Prestwick Airport’s ties to the US defence apparatus on and off for the past decade, it was a surprise to hear First Minister John Swinney’s announcement at the weekend that he would “explore” the possibility of banning American military flights from using the airport if it transpires they are involved in strikes in the Middle East.

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: For years, successive SNP administrations, including Mr Swinney’s, have ducked out of awkward geopolitical issues relating to the airport by insisting that it is a commercial organisation run at arm’s length, with no ministers intervening in its commercial decisions. This rote response allowed the Scottish Government to skip around difficult questions during Donald Trump’s first term in the White House, when US military traffic through South Ayrshire skyrocketed, with numerous crews staying at the US president’s Turnberry resort .

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: READ MORE: Prestwick Airport ‘waiving’ fees for US military aircraft An American C-17 seen at Prestwick Airport in 2025 (Picture: John Devlin) | The Scotsman
